query: effect of increased blood pH on protein synthesis in dialysis patients

retrieved_docs.title: Hemodialysis -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) patients needing dialysis have a build-up of uremic retention solutes, like asymmetric dimethylarginine (ADMA), indoxyl sulfate, and p-cresol, that could have proatherogenic properties. Failing kidneys produce lower amounts of cardiovascular disease and atherogenesis inhibitors like renalase, a soluble monoamine oxidase that regulates blood pressure. In addition, dialysis patients experience a state of chronic inflammation. Biomarkers like CRP, fibrinogen, pentraxin 3 (PTX3), interleukin 6 (IL-6), white blood cell count, and the presence of endothelial cells in circulation independently predict mortality in CKD patients as they indicate endothelial dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemodialysis -- Equipment -- Blood Circuit
retrieved_docs.content: Bicarbonate-buffered dialysate is preferred over acetate dialysate, which may lead to peripheral vasodilation and myocardial depression. However, bicarbonate-buffered dialysate still contains 3 to 5 mmol/l of acetate, which may be associated with increased academia, hypotension, and arrhythmia, particularly in critically ill patients. Online blood temperature and volume monitoring automatically adjust parameters using a biofeedback system. An adjustment follows a fall in circulating blood volume in ultrafiltration rate and dialysate [Na+], and blood temperature is maintained at a target value by thermal transfer control to and from the dialysate to prevent vasodilation and a drop in vascular resistance. High-dialysate calcium (1.75 mmol/l) contributed to greater hemodynamic stability in ESRD patients with cardiomyopathy undergoing intermittent hemodialysis. However, its use in the ICU setting is limited by the occurrence of hypercalcemia. Slower fluid and solute removal in lower efficiency modalities of acute RRT have improved hemodynamic stability with better blood pressure maintenance and reduced vasopressor requirements in patients undergoing continuous rather than intermittent hemodialysis. Further improvements in the design of dialyzers with a sharp cut-off membrane between low-molecular-weight proteins (LMWPs) and albumin, in addition to an adsorptive property for some LMWPs, need to be made. In addition, biocompatible membranes with lower complement cascade activation are necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemodialysis -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Dialysis patients develop arterial sclerosis and secondary stiffening that commonly causes isolated systolic hypertension with raised pulse pressure. Intradialytic hypertension occurs in more chronically volume-overloaded patients with intradialytic vascular resistance surges attributable to acute changes in endothelial cell function during dialysis. Reducing dialysate sodium and including poorly dialyzed antihypertensives like carvedilol in the prescription cause improvement. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ammonia -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In chronic kidney diseases (CKD), the kidney cannot produce and excrete an adequate quantity of ammonia, leading to acid retention and metabolic acidosis. [5] With the progression of kidney disease, the glomerular filtration rate simultaneously falls, leading to increased production and excretion of ammonia by the remaining functioning nephrons. Subsequently, the remaining functioning nephrons cannot sustain the gradual increase of dietary acid load, leading to excessive acid retention inside the body. [6] In CKD, the kidney cannot take in or metabolize glutamine, the substrate for ammonia production. Glutamine uptake and metabolism contribute to only about 35% of ammonia production. The rest comes from other amino acids derived from the breakdown of peptide linkages. Further studies show that glutamine supplementation can increase the formation of ammonia in normal individuals but not in patients with CKD, although the serum level of glutamine is high in both cases. This unique phenomenon in the case of CKD exists due to the reduction of glutamine transporter SNAT3/Slc38a3. [4] Studies performed in nephrectomized rats show that other defects can be seen in the production of ammonia and transport. Researchers found in the animal model of CKD that despite the urinary acidification, the defect was in the net excretion amount of acid. In comparison to normal control, it was also seen that the delivery of ammonia shows a marked elevation at the peripheral accessible portion of the proximal renal tubule. Research also observed that ammonia is at a lower concentration in the loop of Henle, which allows for the escape of more ammonia mainly from the cortex of the nephron, and it then enters back in the renal vein and returns to the central circulation. This property decreases the amount of ammonia in the medullary interstitium, leading to a decreased concentration gradient between the medullary interstitium and the collecting duct lumen. This specific defect of luminal entrapment of ammonia in the collecting duct is believed to correlate with distal delivery of bicarbonate that leads to increased reabsorption of bicarbonate, reduction of formation of titratable acid, and secretion of ammonia. Recent studies in the polycystic kidney model show that the decrease in ammonia excretion in urine is due to the decrease of ammonia transporter called RhCG. However, this hypothesis has been refuted with the findings achieved in the remnant kidney, which shows that the distribution of RhCG transporter protein increases in the apical and basolateral portions. So, in patients with chronic kidney disease, despite the presence of acidosis, the production and excretion of ammonia are seen to be reduced. Thus, normal acid-base balance is disrupted in the case of chronic kidney diseases. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Dialysis Disequilibrium Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Dialysis disequilibrium has been recognized for more than a half-century, but the pathogenesis is not clearly understood. Below are the two commonly proposed theories: The reverse urea effect: Small solutes like urea are rapidly removed during hemodialysis, particularly in the setting of marked azotemia. Urea is usually considered an “ineffective” osmole because of its free diffusion across cell membranes. However, its natural diffusion lags behind the rapid removal of urea from the serum during hemodialysis, creating a transient osmotic gradient between plasma and brain cells. This leads to water shift into brain cells, producing cerebral edema. Cerebral edema is further enhanced by the adaptive increase of brain aquaporin channels and the decrease of urea channels in advanced chronic kidney disease. [10] [11] Studies in rat models of DDS demonstrated that brain-to-plasma urea gradient is increased after hemodialysis and was associated with increased brain water content. [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Pharmacokinetics -- Issues of Concern -- Protein Binding
retrieved_docs.content: These proteins may fluctuate depending on the age and development of the patient, any underlying liver or kidney disease, or nutrition status. One example in which this is relevant is renal failure. In renal failure, uremia decreases the ability of acidic drugs, such as diazepam, to bind to serum proteins. Even though the same amount of drug is initially given, there is far more drug in the "active" space, unbound by serum protein. This will increase the effect of the medication and increase the possibility of toxicity, eg, respiratory depression. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Total Parenteral Nutrition -- Mechanism of Action -- Proteins
retrieved_docs.content: A solution that contains essential and non-essential amino acids except arginine and glutamine Healthy adult requirements are 0.8 to 1 gm of protein/kg/day. This change is based on the condition of the patient. Critically ill patients require 1.5 gm/kg/day, patients with chronic renal failure are given 0.6 to .0.8 gm/kg/day, and patients with acute hepatic encephalopathy need temporary protein restriction to 0.8 gm/kg/day, patients on hemodialysis need 1.2 to 1.3 gm/kg/day. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Disease in Monoclonal Gammopathies -- Treatment / Management -- Extracorporeal Removal of Light Chains
retrieved_docs.content: High-cutoff hemodialysis (HCO-HD) can also be used as another method of extracorporeal removal of FLCs based on their molecular weight. This technique uses a large-pore membrane hemofilter (10 nm) for repeated dialysis sessions over several weeks to remove FLCs. An in vitro study used mathematic modeling of light-chain production and metabolism and revealed that an HCO-HD membrane could remove 90% of FLCs over 3 weeks. [74] One retrospective study evaluated HCO-HD in conjunction with chemotherapy and found 63% of patients achieved dialysis independence. Albumin is lost obligatorily with HCO-HD and requires replacement. [68] One randomized controlled trial compared patients with myeloma cast nephropathy treated with bortezomib and dexamethasone, then treated with intensive dialysis and either a high-cutoff dialyzer or conventional dialyzer. The number of dialysis-independent patients was greater in the high-cutoff group, but the difference did not rise to clinical significance (possibly due to low study numbers). A key association with dialysis independence was achieving a sFLC <500 mg/dL after the first chemotherapy, which is considered the cut-off for cast formation. [51] Additional studies are required in this area.

retrieved_docs.title: Dialysis Disequilibrium Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Intracerebral acidosis: Studies have shown that there is a fall of intracellular pH in brain cells with concomitant carbon dioxide (PCO 2 ) retention among patients after initiation of dialysis. The fall in pH causes sodium and potassium that are bound to proteins to disassociate, making them osmotically active. [14] [15] Additional contributing factors to intracerebral acidosis are the adaptive retention of brain organic osmolytes like glutamine, glutamate, taurine, and myoinositol. Retention of these osmolytes causes a paradoxical fall of intracellular pH leading to increased brain osmolality and cerebral edema. [10] [12] [14] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemodialysis -- Equipment -- Blood Circuit
retrieved_docs.content: Before the delivery of the dialysate to the dialyzer, correction for temperature by heating to 35–38 C followed by exposure to negative pressure for degassing is performed. Special attention must be paid to the osmolality of the dialysis solution, as a severely hyperosmolar solution can cause hypernatremia and other electrolyte disturbances. At the same time, a hypoosmolar solution can lead to rapid hemolysis, severe hyponatremia, and hyperkalemia. An alarm indicates any disturbance in the conductivity of the dialysate beyond 12 to 16 mS/cm and diverts the dialysate to the drain. Cold dialysate (less than 35 C) can cause hypothermia in an unconscious patient, while shivering occurs in a conscious patient. Dialysate temperature of more than 42°C causes blood protein denaturation and hemolysis.

retrieved_docs.title: Erythropoietin Stimulating Agents -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: ACE inhibitors: In a study of 660 hemodialysis patients, ACE inhibitor therapy was associated with lower hemoglobin levels (p=0.02) and higher EPO resistance in those with the ACE gene D/D genotype (p=0.006). Additional research is required. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Dialysis Disequilibrium Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: For patients new to dialysis, short two-hour sessions at 150-200ml/min or sustained low-efficiency dialysis can be done, which can be followed by consecutive dialysis sessions in the following days. [24] The blood flow and dialysate flow can be simultaneously increased in small amounts if patients do not develop DDS during the first session. Blood flow can be further increased to match outpatient settings in successive treatments. Considerations should be given to inpatient initiation of dialysis in cases where BUN is > 100mg/dl or in patients with neurological symptoms such as altered mental status or myoclonus.

retrieved_docs.title: Molecular Absorbent Recirculating System -- Issues of Concern -- MECHANISM
retrieved_docs.content: Based on the concepts of protein affinity and the difference in the concentration gradient of the solutes between the two compartments of the MARS FLUX dialyzer, the highly permeable membrane facilitates the diffusion of protein-bound and water-soluble toxins into the albumin dialysate. Albumin in the dialysate solution has free binding sites, and its affinity towards hydrophobic toxins in the circulating blood pulls them across the membrane and is thus retained by the dialysate. The MARS flux membrane doesn’t allow proteins larger than 50 kDa (example: growth factors) to diffuse across the membrane. [7] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Function Tests -- Interfering Factors -- BUN
retrieved_docs.content: Serum urea/BUN concentrations may also be raised in the presence of a high-protein diet or with patients using oral corticosteroids.

retrieved_docs.title: End-Stage Renal Disease -- Pathophysiology -- Metabolic acidosis
retrieved_docs.content: Induces net negative nitrogen and protein balance and triggers chronic inflammation, which can promote protein catabolism Inhibits osteoblast and osteoclast stimulation Likely worsens hyperparathyroidism Interferes with vitamin D metabolism and worsens hyperkalemia [35] [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Maple Syrup Urine Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment of Acute Metabolic Decompensation
retrieved_docs.content: Residual BCKAD activity and liberation of leucine from catabolism determine the risk of metabolic crisis. [13] Patients with a higher residual BCKAD activity have a better tolerance for leucine. Furthermore, during an illness, these patients face less severe elevations in leucine. The main aim of therapy is to suppress protein catabolism and promote protein synthesis. [3] Management strategies in more severe cases include: Treating the underlying stressor causing the metabolic crisis Restricting protein intake for 24 to 72 hours Providing ample caloric support Providing adequate hydration to maintain metabolic homeostasis Providing supplementation with cofactors Eliminating toxic metabolites Treating associated clinical sequelae Correcting metabolic abnormalities Using dialysis (peritoneal and hemodialysis) in the most severe or acute cases refractory to other treatment

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1961
retrieved_docs.content: Hemodialysis is linked to a spectrum of acute and chronic complications. Acute disorder of hemodialysis and First use syndrome of dialysis both arise as direct consequences of hemodialysis and are characterized by a sudden‑onset clinical course. The dialysis‑related condition described as متلازمة اختلال غسيل الكلى (dialysis‑syndrome) is also caused by hemodialysis and shares this abrupt clinical presentation. Clinically, hemodialysis is associated with chronic hypertension, اختلال وظيفة الكلى (renal functional impairment), فقر دم صغير الكريات (microcytic anemia), and acute renal failure (قصور كلوي حاد). Hemodialysis can also precipitate low blood pressure (ضغط الدم المنخفض). In addition, Sudden acquired retinal degeneration is noted to have a sudden‑onset clinical course, illustrating that abrupt onset is a common qualifier for several severe medical events. These interrelationships underscore the importance of vigilant monitoring for both systemic and organ‑specific adverse effects in patients receiving hemodialysis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1080
retrieved_docs.content: Hemodialysis is a renal replacement therapy that can precipitate several acute and chronic complications. Acute disorder of hemodialysis arises as a direct consequence of hemodialysis and typically follows a sudden‑onset clinical course. Likewise, first use syndrome of dialysis, also caused by hemodialysis, presents with a sudden‑onset clinical course and is synonymous with dialysis disequilibrium syndrome (متلازمة اختلال غسيل الكلى). Hemodialysis is clinically associated with chronic hypertension, disorders of kidney function (اختلال وظيفة الكلى), microcytic anemia (فقر دم صغير الكريات), and acute renal failure (قصور كلوي حاد). In addition, hemodialysis may be linked to low blood pressure (ضغط الدم المنخفض), as reflected in its SSC (systemic side‑effect) relationship. The concept of sudden onset serves as a contextual qualifier for the clinical courses of several conditions, including hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension, dialysis disequilibrium syndrome, and sudden acquired retinal degeneration. Hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension is interpreted through blood pressure measurements and is clinically similar to malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis. Blood pressure itself is related to low blood pressure, and it is a key component of comprehensive cardiovascular evaluation protocols such as tilt‑table testing with continuous ECG and intermittent blood pressure monitoring. Together, these interconnections highlight that hemodialysis can trigger acute syndromes with rapid onset, exacerbate chronic hypertensive states, and affect hematologic and renal parameters, underscoring the importance of vigilant blood pressure monitoring and cardiovascular assessment in patients undergoing dialysis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2888
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with anemia (فقر الدم), as the sudden loss of renal function often leads to reduced production of erythropoietin and impaired clearance of uremic toxins, which together contribute to a decrease in red blood cell count. This relationship highlights the importance of monitoring hemoglobin levels in patients with acute renal injury and addressing anemia promptly to improve oxygen delivery, reduce cardiovascular strain, and support overall recovery. Effective management of both conditions is essential for optimizing patient outcomes in the setting of acute kidney dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2463
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic kidney disease is a renal disorder that falls within the broader category of nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome, indicating its relationship to inflammatory and protein‑losing kidney conditions. A specific form of this disease, chronic kidney disease due to hypertension, is recognized as a subtype of chronic kidney disease, highlighting the role of sustained high blood pressure as an underlying cause. Among the clinical manifestations of chronic kidney disease is عدم تحمل بروتين اللايسين, an intolerance to leucine‑containing proteins, which can contribute to the disease’s symptom profile and influence dietary management strategies. Understanding chronic kidney disease’s classification, its hypertensive subtype, and its protein intolerance manifestation is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted treatment, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 499
retrieved_docs.content: Hemodialysis is linked to a range of acute and chronic complications that often present with a sudden‑onset clinical course. Acute disorder of hemodialysis arises as a direct consequence of the dialysis procedure itself and typically follows a rapid, abrupt clinical trajectory. A similar pattern is seen with the first‑use syndrome of dialysis, another complication that occurs when patients are exposed to hemodialysis for the first time; this syndrome also manifests suddenly after initiation of treatment. Both of these entities are closely related to dialysis‑associated disequilibrium syndrome (متلازمة اختلال غسيل الكلى), which is characterized by an abrupt onset and is explicitly described as having a sudden clinical course. In addition to these acute presentations, hemodialysis is clinically associated with several chronic conditions. Patients undergoing long‑term dialysis frequently exhibit chronic hypertension, persistent impairment of kidney function (اختلال وظيفة الكلى), and microcytic anemia (فقر دم صغير الكريات). Moreover, hemodialysis can precipitate or coexist with acute renal failure (قصور كلوي حاد), underscoring the potential for severe renal compromise in this population. Beyond renal‑related sequelae, the concept of sudden onset is also applied to other ophthalmic pathology, such as sudden acquired retinal degeneration, which similarly follows an abrupt clinical course, highlighting that rapid symptom emergence is a common feature across diverse medical conditions. Overall, the interconnections among hemodialysis, its acute syndromes (acute disorder of hemodialysis, first‑use syndrome, dialysis disequilibrium syndrome), associated chronic disorders (chronic hypertension, kidney function disorder, microcytic anemia, acute renal failure), and the broader pattern of sudden‑onset clinical courses emphasize the importance of vigilant monitoring and prompt management of both immediate and long‑term complications in patients receiving hemodialysis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2129
retrieved_docs.content: Unspecified dialysis (غسيل الكلى غير محدد) frequently co‑occurs with anemia (فقر الدم), indicating that patients receiving dialysis are commonly affected by low hemoglobin levels. Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is classified as a specific type of anemia, making it a subcategory of فقر الدم. Consequently, the presence of dialysis‑related anemia underscores the need to monitor and treat various forms of anemia, including acute posthemorrhagic anemia, in patients undergoing dialysis to improve clinical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes an integrated clinical framework linking abdominal surgical interventions, renal replacement therapies, kidney diseases, hematologic disorders, metabolic and autoimmune conditions, and associated diagnostic and procedural practices. Abdominal destructive procedures, including excision and lysis of peritoneal adhesions, are performed for acute abdomen or palpable abdominal mass and involve the peritoneal surface, which may also present with abdominal hernia or intestinal obstruction. Renal replacement access is created by external arteriovenous shunts for hemodialysis, a modality of dialysis used in chronic kidney disease (CKD), chronic renal failure, end‑stage renal disease (ESRD), and acute renal failure, and is associated with hypertension (chronic, secondary), microcytic anemia, and other blood disorders. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 1071
retrieved_docs.content: Hemodialysis is classified within the Medical & Physical Treatment (PsycINFO Subcluster Term) as a therapeutic modality used for renal replacement. It is directly responsible for several acute and early‑use complications. Both the Acute disorder of hemodialysis and the First use syndrome of dialysis are described as conditions that arise from hemodialysis and share a Sudden onset (contextual qualifier) (qualifier value) clinical course. The Sudden onset (contextual qualifier) (qualifier value) itself is linked to the clinical course of متلازمة اختلال غسيل الكلى (dialysis disequilibrium syndrome), indicating that this syndrome typically presents abruptly after the initiation of hemodialysis. Dialysis disequilibrium syndrome is therefore understood as a complication caused by hemodialysis rather than a cause of it. In addition to these acute complications, hemodialysis is clinically associated with chronic hypertension (hypertension chronic) and فقر دم صغير الكريات (microcytic anemia),

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1995
retrieved_docs.content: Dehydration (التجفاف) is clinically associated with the development of acute kidney injury (فشل كلوي حاد). In turn, acute kidney injury is clinically linked to low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), reflecting the importance of adequate intravascular volume in preserving renal function. Acute kidney injury also encompasses the condition of extracellular blood nitrogen (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية), indicating that disturbances in nitrogenous waste handling outside the kidneys are a relevant component of its clinical spectrum. Understanding these interconnections—how dehydration can precipitate acute kidney injury, how hypovolemia contributes to its progression, and how extracellular nitrogen abnormalities manifest—helps clinicians identify risk factors, monitor patients, and implement timely interventions to protect renal function.

retrieved_docs.content: of acute renal failure (الفشل الكلوي الحاد). Together, these interconnections illustrate how hemodialysis, its acute and chronic complications, related comorbidities, specific glomerular pathologies, systemic autoimmune or drug‑induced syndromes, and targeted ultrasonographic evaluation of the urinary system are integral to understanding renal pathology and guiding comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2686
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney injury, acidosis, hyperkalemia, and extrarenal blood nitrogen are closely interrelated. Acute kidney injury is clinically associated with both acidosis and hyperkalemia, indicating that patients with sudden loss of renal function frequently develop metabolic acidosis and elevated serum potassium levels. In addition, acidosis and hyperkalemia commonly co‑occur, reflecting shared pathophysiological mechanisms in renal failure. Acute kidney injury also includes the condition termed extrarenal blood nitrogen, a manifestation of nitrogenous waste accumulation outside the kidneys. Recognizing these linked abnormalities is essential for timely diagnosis and comprehensive management of patients with acute kidney injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2143
retrieved_docs.content: Acute disorder of hemodialysis and First use syndrome of dialysis are both dialysis‑related conditions that share a Sudden onset (contextual qualifier) (qualifier value) as their clinical course. The Sudden onset (contextual qualifier) (qualifier value) itself is identified as the clinical course of متلازمة اختلال غسيل الكلى (dialysis dysfunction syndrome). Together, these relationships emphasize that abrupt presentation is a defining feature of several hemodialysis complications, underscoring the need for rapid recognition and intervention in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2823
retrieved_docs.content: Hemodialysis is a principal procedure related to the urinary system, serving as a major renal replacement therapy within urinary system interventions. Clinical experience shows that patients undergoing hemodialysis frequently present with chronic hypertension, indicating a strong association between hemodialysis and hypertension chronic. In addition, hemodialysis is commonly linked with فقر دم صغير الكريات (microcytic anemia), reflecting another prevalent comorbidity in this patient population. Recognizing the interconnectedness of hemodialysis, chronic hypertension, and microcytic anemia is essential for comprehensive patient assessment, monitoring, and management in renal care.

Collectively, these relationships demonstrate how hemodialysis can precipitate acute, sudden‑onset disorders and chronic complications, how necrotizing glomerulonephritis represents a severe, systemic glomerular disease, and how renal functional impairment, specific glomerular lesions, autoimmune conditions, drug‑induced reactions, and bleeding‑related anemias intersect within medical classification systems to inform comprehensive patient assessment and coordinated care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 61
retrieved_docs.content: Hemodialysis is a specific modality of renal dialysis that functions as a parent procedure for various therapeutic and diagnostic concepts. It is directly linked to acute kidney failure, being listed as a member of the acute renal failure syndrome (الفشل الكلوي الحاد (SMQ)) and having a formal relationship with acute renal insufficiency (قصور كلوي حاد). Clinical entities that arise as direct consequences of hemodialysis include the Acute disorder of hemodialysis and the First use syndrome of dialysis; both are described as “due to” hemodialysis and are characterized by a sudden‑onset clinical course, exemplified by the dialysis disequilibrium syndrome (متلازمة اختلال غسيل الكلى). The contextual qualifier “Sudden onset (contextual qualifier) (qualifier value)” is also applied to Sudden acquired retinal degeneration, indicating that the sudden‑onset pattern is a broader descriptor of disease presentation. Hemodialysis is clinically associated with several systemic complications: chronic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2745
retrieved_docs.content: High plasma osmolality, high blood glucose, acute kidney failure, type 2 diabetes, an abnormal psychological state, and low blood volume are closely interrelated clinical entities. High plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels, reflecting the hyperosmolar consequences of uncontrolled diabetes, and it is also linked to type 2 diabetes itself, indicating that patients with this form of diabetes frequently develop increased plasma osmolality. In addition, high plasma osmolality is clinically associated with an abnormal psychological state, consistent with the neurocognitive disturbances that can arise from severe hyperosmolar conditions, and it commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume, suggesting that dehydration or hypovolemia often accompanies the hyperosmolar state. Acute kidney failure is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes, underscoring diabetes as a major risk factor for rapid loss of renal function, and it is also linked to an abnormal psychological state, reflecting the mental status changes that can result from uremic toxins. Moreover, acute kidney failure is clinically associated with low blood volume, indicating that hypovolemia can precipitate or worsen renal injury. Together, these relationships highlight a network in which type 2 diabetes can lead to high plasma osmolality and acute kidney failure, both of which are connected to low blood volume and neuropsychiatric disturbances, emphasizing the importance of monitoring osmolar status, volume balance, renal function, and mental health in patients with diabetes.
